exemplar.title: An Investigation Into The Relative Performance Of Varying Diagnostic Methods for Screening Of Coeliac Disease

detailedExemplarData.title: An Investigation Into The Relative Performance Of Varying Diagnostic Methods for Screening Of Coeliac Disease

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Follows the classical IA sequence with recognizable introduction, modelling and conclusion structure
Uses headings to delineate sections and allows the reader to track the progression of the investigation
Basic organization enables the exploration to be followed from start to finish
weaknesses: Frequent digressions and repeated quotations interrupt logical flow
Misplaced methodological fragments and split tables disrupt seamless structure
Unlabeled figures, formatting errors and unnecessary screenshots reduce conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work in band 2 for coherence and organization but band 1 for conciseness due to irrelevant material and formatting issues. Overall, the exploration shows some coherence and logical development with adequate organization, meriting a score of 2 out of 4.

strengths: Correct use of tree diagrams to visualize conditional outcomes
Overlay of binomial distribution graphs effectively compares variances
Generally intelligible explanations accompany the mathematical representations
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (missing vertical bars, mix of plain text and MathML)
Mislabels events and omits full definitions of acronyms in captions
Incomplete or poorly formatted tables reduce the effectiveness of representations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All communication strands were assessed at band 2: some appropriate mathematical language and notation, some representations, and mostly clear explanations with inconsistencies. These qualities justify a score of 2 out of 4.

strengths: Rationale clearly communicates personal interest in a medical context
Selection of a real clinical study shows initiative in topic choice
weaknesses: Analysis follows standard textbook procedures with little originality
Limited independent mathematical thinking beyond routine application
Personal approach does not significantly drive novel exploration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each strand was placed in band 1: limited evidence of independent thinking, some personal approach, and minimal creativity. The work shows personal interest but lacks distinctive personal direction, warranting 1 out of 3.

strengths: Occasional linkage of results back to the stated aim
Descriptive commentary highlights basic comparisons of probabilities
weaknesses: Reflection is mostly descriptive with minimal probing of model assumptions
Lacks discussion of parameter uncertainty and limitations
No deep critical analysis of methodological robustness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection strands all fall in band 1: limited connection to understanding, basic analysis, and superficial depth. The minimal critical reflection merits 1 out of 3.

strengths: Appropriate selection of Bayesâ theorem, conditional probability tables and binomial distributions
Correct conversion of prevalence into probability and clear binomial formula application
Effective use of technology (Excel BINOM.DIST) for large computations
weaknesses: Faulty Bayesâ theorem rearrangement and algebraic errors in sensitivity/PPV derivation
Inconsistent rounding practices leading to calculation discrepancies
Some union and probability values do not reconcile with earlier findings
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Use-of-Mathematics strands span band 2 and 3 (relevant course-level mathematics with partial correctness and good understanding in parts). The presence of significant but non-pervasive errors and solid conceptual grasp supports a midpoint score of 3 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Apart from choosing a personally interesting medical context, little independent mathematical thinking is displayed; the work largely follows textbook probability procedures.  Evidence of originality is therefore limited, consistent with band 1.

The student demonstrates a sound grasp of how sensitivity, specificity, PPV/NPV and prevalence interplay, and how binomial models reflect repeated trials.  Although not rigorous, the explanations reveal good conceptual understanding in parts, matching band 3.

Numerical work with the binomial model is mostly correct, but the earlier rearrangement of Bayesâ theorem is faulty and rounded counts are inconsistently handled.  These significant yet non-pervasive errors suggest âpartially correctâ mathematicsâband 2.

The exploration is lengthy and contains sizable amounts of background quotation and partially-filled tables that do not feed the subsequent mathematics.  Unnecessary screenshots of spreadsheet formulae and GDC windows add volume without insight.  Consequently the piece is only partly relevant and far from concise, placing it in band 1 for this strand.

Probability symbols, conditional notation and factorial/binomial notation are used and generally recognisable.  Nevertheless, several mis-types (e.g. $P(T^{+}D^{+})$ labelled as sensitivity, missing vertical bars) and inconsistent use of bold/plain italics show only âsomeâ appropriate language, matching band 2.

Explanations are usually intelligible, but symbol/key mismatches and occasional algebraic slips (e.g. mishandled Bayes rearrangement) introduce inconsistency.  Overall clarity is acceptable but not sustained, so band 2 (âmostly clear with some consistencyâ) is most fitting.

Bayesâ theorem, conditional probability tables, tree diagrams and binomial distributions are all SL-appropriate and relevant to evaluating diagnostic accuracy.  Their selection fits the task, indicating use of ârelevant mathematics at course levelâ, i.e., band 3.

The student includes tables of parameters, $2\times2$ outcome grids, tree diagrams and probability-mass graphs.  While these do support the discussion, several are incomplete or poorly formatted and the links to the narrative are weak.  Hence the work displays âsome appropriate representationsâ rather than effective or sophisticated use.

Reflection is mostly descriptive (e.g., repeating that probabilities are âhigherâ or âlowerâ); there is minimal probing of why the model might be inappropriate (e.g., independence assumptions, sampling bias).  Some reflection exists but is limited in depth, aligning with band 1.

Using Bayesâ theorem and binomial modelling in a clinical context shows a little initiative, yet both techniques are standard syllabus material and are applied in a routine fashion.  Creativity is therefore limited, justifying band 1.

The report follows the classical IA sequence (rationale â background â modelling â results â conclusion) and the investigation can be followed from start to finish.  However, numerous digressions, repeated quotations, and misplaced methodological fragments (e.g., truncated tables, dangling figure references) interrupt the logical flow.  Overall coherence is intermittent rather than continuous, so the work sits best in the âsome coherence and logical developmentâ band rather than the fully coherent bands above.

Critical analysis is confined to comparing two numerical success probabilities and noting variance differences.  There is no evaluation of rounding impacts, misuse of Bayes, or parameter uncertainty, so only basic analysis is presentâband 1.

The student occasionally links results back to the stated aim (identifying HLA-DQ2 as preferable), but the connection to mathematical understanding of diagnostic testing remains superficial.  Consequently the work shows a limited connection, deserving band 1.

A basic structure (introduction/body/conclusion) is evident and headings are used, yet within sections the order of material is often disjointed (e.g., Bayes rearrangement appears before notation is finalised; figures are introduced long after discussion).  Some tables are split across pages and captions are repeated, showing only adequate rather than clear or seamless organisation.

The student does outline a personal rationale and selects data from a chosen study, but the subsequent analysis is generic and not obviously driven by a distinctive personal direction.  This represents âsome evidenceâ rather than a clear personal approach, hence band 1.

annotations.comment: Caption uses abbreviations (âSp, S, PPVâ) without defining them. Use full parameter names to avoid ambiguity for readers unfamiliar with the acronyms.
Adopting a global prevalence for a specific clinical sample may violate assumptions. Justify why the global 1.4% rate is applicable to the Ullrich study population.
The text explanation mislabels events A and B as âdependentâ yet treats them as mutually exclusive. Clarify event relationships and correct terminology.
The title contains a typo (âVarvingâ instead of âVaryingâ), which distracts from the professional presentation. Correct the wording to enhance precision and credibility.
The rationale effectively communicates personal interest in epidemiology and outlines a clear aim to compare diagnostic tests using Bayesâ theorem and binomial modelling.
Background section relies heavily on quoted text and lengthy descriptions that are not directly used later. Paraphrase and condense to maintain conciseness and relevance.
Figure 1âs caption indicates missing information (âGIVE INFO ON THE ARAMETERSâ¦â). Complete parameter definitions and ensure notation is introduced before the table.
The notation table uses a mix of inline MathML and plain text inconsistently. Standardize mathematical notation (e.g., always use $$P(T^+|D^+)$$) for clarity.
Headings such as âIgG AGA/TTGâ appear without context or transitions. Reorganize the Methods section to introduce each test method before listing values.
Table 4 presents confidence intervals but the analysis later uses only point estimates. Discuss parameter uncertainty (CIs) when reflecting on model robustness.
Conversion from 1.4% to 0.014 is correct. Well done on accurately translating the global prevalence into a usable probability.
The Bayesâ theorem statement is reversed: $$P(T^+|D^+)=\frac{P(D^+|T^+)P(T^+)}{P(D^+)}$$ should read $$P(D^+|T^+)=\frac{P(T^+|D^+)P(D^+)}{P(T^+)}$$. Correct the formula to avoid conceptual error.
Algebraic manipulation conflates sensitivity and PPV. Sensitivity $0.92=P(T^+|D^+)$ not $=\frac{PPV\times P(T^+)}{prevalence}$. Re-evaluate derivation steps.
Recommend delaying rounding until the final result to reduce cumulative rounding error, rather than rounding intermediate counts to integer values early.
The 2Ã2 summary table is rendered blank. Ensure all cells are filled with calculated values to support subsequent tree diagram.
Table on page 6 includes garbled symbols due to formatting errors. Recreate the table clearly to show actual counts and avoid reader confusion.
The tree diagram effectively visualizes all outcomes with computed probabilities, aiding comprehension of conditional events.
In the union calculation, values for $$P(	ext{no CD})$$ and $$P(	ext{with CD})$$ must match earlier NPV and PPV findings: 0.939 and 0.044 respectively, not 0.412. Reconcile these values.
Equation (8) introduces the binomial formula clearly; linking parameters $$n=583$$ and $$p=0.924$$ is precise and prepares for distribution plotting.
Graph axes lack labels and units, making interpretation difficult. Label the xâaxis (number of successes) and yâaxis (probability) for clarity.
Screenshot of spreadsheet formulas in the table is unnecessary. Replace with a concise summary of computed probabilities in tabular form.
Use of Excelâs BINOM.DIST function demonstrates effective use of technology to handle large computations.
Reflect on binomial model assumptions (e.g. trial independence, identical distribution) and discuss their validity in this clinical context.
When computing cumulative probabilities, show both steps: computing P(Xâ¤465) then 1âP to obtain P(Xâ¥466). The final value should be explicit.
The Personal Engagement in selecting a real medical study is clear, but the methodology follows standard steps with limited originality. Consider introducing an alternative parameter or scenario.
The text notes âwhere p = 0â incorrectly; this typographical error should read âwhere p = 0.924â. Proofread to correct parameter values.
The IgG AGA/TTG counts produce $$P(	ext{negative test})=0.977$$ and $$P(	ext{with CD})=0.429$$, yet the actual PPV 0.45 and NPV 0.86 suggest P(success) should recalc; check consistency.
Overlaying both binomial distributions in Figure 11 excellently facilitates direct comparison and highlights differences in variance.
The conclusion restates results but lacks discussion of limitations (e.g., sample bias, CI uncertainty). Add critical reflection to deepen understanding.
Sentence structures in the conclusion are overly long and complex, affecting readability. Break into shorter sentences for greater conciseness.
